coredata.dc:title: Metal organic framework loaded fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanozyme with light regulating redox ability for detection of ferric ion and glutathione
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jun
Gong, Jiawen
Wei, Jingnan
Yang, Qi
Li, Guangjie
Tong, Yuping
He, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: The redox state disorder of biological system caused by oxidative stress can lead to a variety of clinical dysfunction and diseases. It is an important challenge to find artificial materials that can efficiently adjust the redox balance to maintain health. In this work, a nitrogen-doped carbon (NCDs) redox nanozyme loaded into metal organic framework (MOF, UiO-66) is designed to form NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites. The high specific surface area and porosity of UiO-66 serve as ideal carrier to support multifunctional NCDs. NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites are comprehensively investigated for their ability to scavenge or generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radicals. Experimental results demonstrate that NCDs/UiO-66 can remove intrinsic free radicals ( • OH, O2 •− and ABTS•+), exhibiting superoxide dismutase-like activity and antioxidant capability. Moreover, NCDs/UiO-66 can efficiently produce ROS ( • OH, O2 •− and 1O2) under irradiation showing light induced oxidase-like activity and pro-oxidant capability. This suggests the anti-oxidant and pro-oxidant activities of NCDs/UiO-66 could be regulated easily by light irradiation. Using the fluorescent property and light-activated oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66, the methods for detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) and glutathione (GSH) are developed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Carbon dots
Light regulating
Antioxidant
Biological detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antioxidant capability in scavenging reactive oxygen species and free radicals
Light-induced generation of reactive oxygen species

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the procedure to prepare NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites.
(a) TEM image of NCDs. (b) SEM image of UiO-66. (c) Photographs of UiO-66, NC/UiO-1, NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-3 suspensions. TEM images of (d-f) NC/UiO-1, (g-i) NC/UiO-2 and (j-l) NC/UiO-3 nanocomposites.

(a) XPS survey and (b) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s signal of NC/UiO-3 nanocomposite. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of UiO-66, NC/UiO-1, NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-3, the emission spectra were recorded in an aqueous solution at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (d) UV–VIS spectra of UiO-66, NC/UiO-1, NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-3 suspensions.

Antioxidant activity of NCDs/UiO-66 hybrid nanostructures to scavenge •OH, O2 •− and ABTS•+ free radicals. (a) UV–Vis spectra and (b) absorbance of solution containing H2O2, Pt NPs and TMB with different concentrations of NC/UiO-3. (c) Hydroxyl radical consuming ability of different samples. The UV–Vis spectra (d) and absorbance at 525 nm (e) of NBT solution containing superoxide produced by KO2 system in the presence of SOD and NC/UiO-3 with different concentration. (f) Superoxide radical consuming activity of different samples. The UV–Vis spectra (g) and absorbance at 650 nm (h) of ABTS•+ radicals solutions treated by NC/UiO-3 with different concentration. (i) ABTS•+ consuming activity of different samples.

Light-activated oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites. (a) UV–Vis spectra of TMB solutions in the absence and presence of NCDs, NC/UiO-1, NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-3 after 20 min irradiation by > 420 nm light. (b) Effects of light source on the oxidase-like activity of NC/UiO-3 nanocomposites. (c) Effects of pH on the oxidase-like activity. (d) Effects of concentration of NC/UiO-3 on the oxidase-like activity. (e) Effect of the concentration of TMB on the reaction rate. (f) Results of the fitted double reciprocal curve according to Michaelis − Menten’s Equation for the calculation of enzyme kinetic parameters.

Generation of reactive oxygen species from NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites under irradiation. ESR spectra obtained from the (a) control samples containing spin trap alone under light or the sample containing spin trap and catalysts without irradiation, and the (b) samples containing catalysts + spin traps (25 mM DMPO or 0.5 mM TEMP) + irradiation. (c) Mechanisms of generating or consuming reactive oxygen species by NCDs/UiO-66 under light and dark conditions.

(a) The intensity of the fluorescence emission peak of NC/UiO-3 mixed with different cations. (b) The intensity of the fluorescence emission peak of the mixed solution of NC/UiO-3 and different cations. (c) In the range of 0 ∼ 0.1 mM (d) The relationship diagram of the fluorescence emission peak intensity of different concentrations of Fe3+ at 440 nm.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oxidized TMB in the presence, absence or different concentrations of glutathione (0 ∼ 40 μM). (b) Linear fitting is performed in the concentration range of 0 ∼ 15 μM. (c) Absorbance at 652 nm dependent on concentrations of glutathione. (d) The selectivity of NCDs/UiO-66 sensors. The concentration of all amino acids tested was 15 μM. Each error bar shows the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

head.ce:title: Metal organic framework loaded fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanozyme with light regulating redox ability for detection of ferric ion and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Materials for Energy Storage and Conversion of Henan Province, Institute of Surface Micro and Nano Materials, College of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Xuchang University, Xuchang, Henan 461000, China

College of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors.at: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Materials for Energy Storage and Conversion of Henan Province, School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Institute of Surface Micro and Nanomaterials, Xuchang University, Xuchang, Henan 461000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Materials for Energy Storage and Conversion of Henan Province
School of Chemical and Materials Engineering
Institute of Surface Micro and Nanomaterials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The redox state disorder of biological system caused by oxidative stress can lead to a variety of clinical dysfunction and diseases. It is an important challenge to find artificial materials that can efficiently adjust the redox balance to maintain health. In this work, a nitrogen-doped carbon (NCDs) redox nanozyme loaded into metal organic framework (MOF, UiO-66) is designed to form NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites. The high specific surface area and porosity of UiO-66 serve as ideal carrier to support multifunctional NCDs. NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites are comprehensively investigated for their ability to scavenge or generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radicals. Experimental results demonstrate that NCDs/UiO-66 can remove intrinsic free radicals ( • OH, O2 •− and ABTS•+), exhibiting superoxide dismutase-like activity and antioxidant capability. Moreover, NCDs/UiO-66 can efficiently produce ROS ( • OH, O2 •− and 1O2) under irradiation showing light induced oxidase-like activity and pro-oxidant capability. This suggests the anti-oxidant and pro-oxidant activities of NCDs/UiO-66 could be regulated easily by light irradiation. Using the fluorescent property and light-activated oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66, the methods for detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) and glutathione (GSH) are developed.

ce:section.ce:para: Redox balance plays crucial role for maintaining the life health. The redox state disorder of biological system caused by oxidative stress can lead to a variety of clinical dysfunction and diseases [1,2]. The overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is recognized as the direct cause to oxidative stress. On other side, ROS can promote the synthesis of hormones and function in cell signal transduction, energy synthesis and growth regulation [3–5]. The key lies in regulating the reduction and generation of ROS to keep the redox balance for specific application. Severe oxidative stress requires external intervention to restore equilibrium, and the effective method is use of redox enzymes or antioxidants [6]. However, most natural enzymes and antioxidants can only influence unidirectionally on pro-oxidant or antioxidant reactions [7]. Therefore, seeking the ideal agents with biocompatibility and adjustable ability to scavenge/generate ROS is highly required and urgently.

Nanozymes, the nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity, have been reported to show potential in regulating ROS because of their multiple oxidoreductase properties [8–10]. For example, Pt nanoparticles (NPs) can exhibit four type enzyme-like activity, including oxidase, peroxidase, superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase. Therefore, they can remove superoxide and H2O2 as well as generate hydroxyl radicals in the interaction with H2O2 under acidic condition [11]. The multiple enzyme-like activity endows them with antioxidant and pro-oxidant function at the same time. Recently, light-responsive nanozymes have been proposed in some pioneer studies [12–14]. Carbon dots and their composite materials has been applied widely in many fields due to their plentiful raw materials, facile synthesis and functional process, unique optical property and abundant functional groups [15]. Chong [16] et al. found that graphene quantum dot (GQD) can effectively scavenge reactive free radicals and protect cells from oxidative damage. When exposed to blue light, GQD produces phototoxicity by increasing the level of intracellular ROS and reducing cell viability. The light induced crossover of antioxidant and pro-oxidant activity on carbon nanomaterials were also demonstrated by other groups [17,18], which provide a facile way to tune the production of ROS via irradiation. However, the carbon dot is readily difficult to collect and purify because of their extremely small size, which will limit their practical application.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), structured from organic ligands and inorganic metal ions, are notable for their outstanding crystallinity, diverse structures, large surface areas, adsorption performance, and excellent component tunability. The water stability of MOFs is a key requisite for their possible actual applications in separation, catalysis, adsorption, and other water environment remediation areas [19,20]. Especially, it can be used as a carrier to support smaller particle size and provide more active sites for catalytic reaction and reduce the transport and electron transfer paths in reaction [21–23]. Motivated by above ideas, UiO-66 supported fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dot (NCDs/UiO-66) nanozyme composites were designed in this study. The primary objective is to study the regulation of redox ability to scavenge and generate ROS, aiming at realizing the controllable transformation of pro-oxidant and anti-oxidant activities through switch of dark/light conditions. In addition, as a proof of concept to explore the multiple functions, the method for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and glutathione are developed by using the fluorescent response and light-induced oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 composites.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, analytical reagent ≥ 99.8%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, analytical reagent ≥ 30%), 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%), nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%), zirconiun tetrachloride (ZrCl4, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, analytical reagent ≥ 99.5%), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·6H2O, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%), salicylic acid (C7H6O3, analytical reagent ≥ 99.5%), glutathione (C10H17N3O6S, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%), L(+)-Cysteine (C3H7NO2S, analytical reagent ≥ 98.5%), glucose (C6H12O6·H2O, analytical reagent), glycine (C2H5NO2, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%), l-histidine (C6H9N3O2, analytical reagent) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd (Shanghai, China), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonicacid) (ABTS, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%), 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, analytical reagent ≥ 97.0%), methyl sulfoxide (DMSO, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, analytical reagent ≥ 99.5%), blue tetrazolium (C40H32C12N8O2, analytical reagent ≥ 95%), uric acid (C5H4N4O3, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%) were commercially available from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (CA, USA). 1,4-dicarboxybenzene (C8H6O4, analytical reagent ≥ 98.0%) and 18-crown-6 (C12H24O6, analytical reagent ≥ 99.0%) were commercially available from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (India). Milli-Q water (18 MΩ•cm) was used for all the experimental preparations.

A hydrothermal method was used to prepare the nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCDs). First, 1.05 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Then the mixture was blended with 0.335 mL of ethylenediamine. After magnetic stirring for 30 min, transfer the mixture to a 25 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C, reacting for 5 h. The obtained brown-black solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for 10 min to remove the large particles.

UiO-66 was synthesized according to the method in the published literature [21], but with some changes. First, 116.6 mg of ZrCl4 and 83.1 mg of H2BDC are dissolved in 10 mL of DMF respectively. Then 4 mL of acetic acid solution is added to the mixture, which is subsequently magnetically stirred at room temperature for 20 min. Next, the mixed solution is transferred to a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, sealed and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the white precipitate was collected and washed once with DMF, and three times with absolute ethanol by centrifugation. The obtained UiO-66 was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for further use.

10 mg of UiO-66 was dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water. Then different volumes of nitrogen-doped carbon suspension were added and the mixture was stirred vigorously for 12 h. Then the product (NCDs/UiO-66) was collected and centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 5 min, washed twice with water. Next, the precipitate was redispersed in 1 mL deionized water and the NCDs/UiO-66 nanoparticles solution was obtained. Using the same synthesis process, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites with different proportions of NCDs were prepared. The NCDs/UiO-66 products with the dosage of NCDs were 100 μL, 300 μL and 500 μL were labeled by NC/UiO-1, NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-3, respectively.

UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra were obtained using a Cary 5000 UV–VIS-NIR Spectrometer (Varian, USA) and a matched quartz cuvette with a path length of 1 cm. The crystal structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance diffractometer, Bruker, Germany) using monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured on a Tecnai G2 F20 U-TWIN electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using 150 W Al Kα radiation and a base pressure of approximately 3 × 10−9 mbar. The binding energies were calibrated to the C 1 s line at 284.8 eV.

TMB was used as a chromogenic substrate to determine the oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 hybrid nanomaterials. 100 μL of NCDs/UiO-66 solution (10 mg/mL) and 30 μL of TMB (20 mM) were mixed into 6 mL of deionized water (or buffers with different pH) were mixed in a glass vial. Then the suspension was stirred continuously in dark for 10 min to ensure it reaches the equilibrium of adsorption and desorption. During the stirring, the circulating cooling water was kept working to control the temperature of the solution. The solutions were placed under a 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 cut-off filter to generate light with λ < 420 nm. 2 mL of the solution was taken out at 5 min interval and centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. Then the UV–Vis spectra was recorded by Varian Cary 5000 spectrophotometer to monitor the degree of TMB oxidation. The enzymatic kinetic parameters were calculated according to Michaelise-Menten equation (1):
(1)
1 / ν =
(
K m
/
V
max
)
×
( 1 /
[ S ]
)
+ 1 /
V
max
where ν is the initial reaction rate, Vmax is the maximum reaction rate, [S] is the substrate concentration, and Km is the Michaelise constant.

A Bruker A300 electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker AG) was used to measure the ESR spectrum. A 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the light source. 50 µL of the sample solution containing a spin trap and an equal amount of control or NCDs/UiO-66 is placed in a quartz capillary and sealed. Then the capillary is inserted into the measurement chamber, and the ESR spectrum is recorded under the specified time of illumination. Under light excitation, the formation of hydroxyl radicals or superoxide radicals in the NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite in 25 mM DMPO aqueous solution and methanol solution were verified. The 0.5 mM TEMP was used to detect the formation of singlet oxygen.

Based on Pt Nano particles (NPs) and H2O2, the scavenge ability to hydroxyl radicals of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite was measured in a non-light environment. TMB was chosen as the indicator. The hydroxyl radicals generated by the reaction of Pt NPs and H2O2 can promote the oxidation of TMB, and the oxidation products have obvious absorption characteristic peaks at 652 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectrum. In this experiment, a solution of 10 μL H2O (0.2 M) and 5 μL Pt NPs (0.512 mM) was mixed with 3 mL of deionized water and 20 μL of catalyst (10 mg/mL) solution to trigger the reaction. During the selected reaction time (3 min), the characteristic peak signal was recorded with an ultraviolet–visible spectrometer.

The KO2 based system was applied to verify the scavenge ability to superoxide radicals of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite. KO2 (25 mM) and 18-crown-6 (1.75 mM) need to be dissolved in DMSO, while 10 mM NBT needs to be dissolved in water. Nitro tetrazolium blue (NBT) works as a probe. When NBT reacts with the superoxide radical generated by KO2, the products have obvious characteristic peaks in the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum. In this experiment, 540 μL of 18-crown-6, 60 μL of KO2, and 50 μL of NCDs/UiO-66 solution were mixed uniformly. When the reaction proceeded for 3 min, 2.6 mL of NBT (10 mM) was added to the mixture. After 5 min, the absorption peak of the mixed solution was recorded using the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum.

7 mM ABTS and 2.44 mM K2S2O8 were dissolved in 10 mL of water. The mixure was kept in dark environment for 20 h and diluted about 60 times before use. The catalysts of different concentrations were mixed with 3 mL ABTS solution, reacting for 5 min. Then the absorption peaks of the specific product were measured with an ultraviolet–visible spectrometer.

NCDs/UiO-66 has the characteristic of fluorescence. which will lead to the fluorescence quenching phenomenon as a salt solution of metal cation is added. In this experiment, twelve salt solutions of metal cations were added: Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ni+, Cu2+, Zn2+. In addition, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2- were introduced to eliminate the interference of the anions on the experimental results.

The NCDs/UiO-66 composite material has an ability to promote oxidation, which could be used to detect the biomolecules of antioxidant, such as Glutathione (GSH). In the colorimetric detection of Glutathione, the solution contains 6 mL of deionized water, 100 μL of NCDs/UiO-66 (10 mg/mL), 40 μL of TMB (20 mM) and different concentrations of GSH solution. A 300 W xenon lamp was used (filter λ > 420 nm) as the light source. The reaction continued for 7 min. Then the characteristic peak curve was recorded with the application of the ultraviolet–visible spectrum. All the above tests for catalytic activity were repeated three times.

Fig. 1 illustrates the procedure for preparation of NCDs/UiO-66 composites. Firstly, UiO-66 and NCDs have been prepared according to previous method [24], respectively. NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine. Fig. 2 shows the image of as-synthesized NCDs and UiO-66. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots are spherical with an average size of Ø 2.40 ± 0.38 nm (Fig. 2a). The synthesized UiO-66 is well dispersed with a uniform octahedral shape and an average diameter of about ∼ 500 nm (Fig. 2b, Fig. S1). NCDs/UiO-66 composites were formed by a facile physical mixing of UiO-66 and NCD suspensions. Benefiting from the high porosity and large specific surface area of UiO-66, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots are easily absorbed or loaded onto UiO-66 to form NCDs/UiO-66 nanostructures. It is obvious that the color of the suspension changed from white to brownish yellow with the increasing loading of NCDs (Fig. 2c). The color is stable against washing and dispersion, which means that the NCDs were firmly attached to UiO-66 [25,26]. The abundant –OH and –COOH groups on the surface allow the NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite with good dispersibility in aqueous solution. The TEM images of NCDs/UiO-66 with different loading amount of NCDs were captured (Fig. 2d-l). Typical morphology of NCDs attached in the surface of UiO-66 were observed in the enlarged images (Fig. 2f, 2i and 2 l), especially for the NC/UiO-3 sample where the NCDs were marked with red arrows (Fig. 2l). The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) element mapping images of NC/UiO-3 is shown in Fig. S2. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis confirmed the existence of element C, O, Zr and N (Fig. 3 a-e). It should be noticed that C in NCDs and UiO-66 is hard to be distinguished. Fig. S3 shows the XRD pattern of pure UiO-66 and NCDs/UiO-66. There seems no evident change in peaks when NCDs are loaded on UiO-66. The surface area was calculated to be 1327.063 m2/g, according to Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) equation and the pore size was tested to be lower than ∼4 nm, based on Barrett-Joyner-Halenda equation.

To further confirm the loading of NCDs on UiO-66, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to analyze the surface composition and chemical valence state of the elements. As shown in Fig. 3a, XPS survey spectra shows the the characteristic peaks from Zr 3d, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, confirming the presence of Zr, C, N and O elements. The atomic percentages of Zr, C, N and O in the sample were calculated to be 3.5%, 60.87%, 7.81% and 27.83%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s is fitted to 4 types of carbon signals, which can be indexed to CC, CC, CO, and CN (Fig. 3b). The N 1 s spectrum is fitted to two characteristic signals with the binding energies of 399.7 and 400.5 eV, which could be attributed to the two nitrogen configurations of pyrrole (N-C3) and quaternary ammonium (NHx). Since the pure UiO-66 did not include element N, this indicates that the UiO-66 surface was coated with abundant NCDs. There are van der Waals bonds in NCDs and the surface functional groups are self-cross linked through hydrogen bonds, thus the surface of the composite material is rich in N element.

Pure NCDs show typical absorption and fluorescent response in UV–Vis region because of unique aromatic conjugation structures, while pure UiO-66 itself has no such response. Thus, the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) and fluorescence emission spectra are useful to characterize the formation and optical property of NCDs/UiO-66 composites. As shown in Fig. 3c and 3d, NCDs shows two typical absorption peaks at 240 nm and 340 nm, and a strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm under excitation (λ = 360 nm). NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites show an absorption and fluorescence emission features similar to the pure NCDs. The intensities of fluorescence (FL) emission peak at 450 nm gradually increases with increasing loading amount of NCDs, which demonstrates again that the nitrogen-doped carbon material successfully attached to the UiO-66 carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant capability in scavenging reactive oxygen species and free radicals
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant capacity of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites is mainly evaluated by scavenging the ROS and ABTS•+ free radicals. Here the hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals were chosen because they are the most representative ROS in biological conditions. Classic Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 is the often-used method to generate •OH. However, Fe2+ could combine with NCDs and depress the Fenton reaction. Therefore, Pt NPs behaving like peroxidase was applied to replace Fe2+, reacting with H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals [25,26]. The hydroxyl radicals then oxidize chromogenic substrate TMB to oxTMB showing typical blue color and absorption. In the absence of NCDs/UiO-66 (control group), a strong absorption peak signal appeared at 652 nm, indicating the formation of •OH. While after the addition of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites, the intensity of the absorption peak decreases obviously and presents a significant concentration and NCDs loading dependence. (Fig. 4 a and 4b). The results confirmed that the NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite can efficiently remove •OH. Among the NCDs/UiO-66 composites with different NCDs loading amount, NC/UiO-3 has the highest scavenging efficiency for •OH, followed by NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-1 (Fig. 4c).

SOD is a natural scavenger of superoxide radicals thus plays crucial role in reduction of oxidative stress [27,28]. In order to verify whether NCDs/UiO-66 composites have the SOD-like activity to reduce superoxide radical, superoxide radicals were generated in situ by KO2 in dimethyl sulfoxide. Molecular probe (NBT) was used to test the production of superoxide. Colorless NBT can react with superoxide radicals (O2 •−) to form blue product with characteristic absorption peak at 545 nm (Fig. 4d). It is found that either SOD or NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite was added, both the color and absorbance significantly decreased, indicating the reduction of superoxide. The superoxide scavenging activity is not only dependent on the concentration of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite, but also on the loading amount of NCDs. The high loading of NCDs results in high SOD-like activity. In comparison, the superoxide scavenging efficiency of 0.078 mg/mL NC/UiO-3 is higher than that of 60 U/mL SOD (Fig. 4f).

The total antioxidant capability of NCDs/UiO is also evaluated by ABTS assay. ABTS•+ is a nitrogen-centered stable free radical that is often used for natural antioxidants evaluation. When NCDs/UiO-66 is added to ABTS•+ solution, the navy-blue color of ABTS•+ immediately fades, indicating the strong activity to scavenge ABTS•+. The scavenging activity is also demonstrated by the significant reduction of the characteristic absorption in UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 4g). The scavenging activity for ABTS•+ seems to indicate intense electron and proton transfer in NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites [29]. It was also found that the ABTS•+ consuming activity of NCDs/UiO-66 is highly dependent on the concentration and NCDs loading amount. NC/UiO-3 has the strongest scavenging ability on ABTS•+ followed by NC/UiO-2 and NC/UiO-1. Such enhanced antioxidant activity may come from: (1) the special structures with large specific surface area and abundant active sites provided by UiO-66 and (2) substantial dispersive NCDs facilitating the transfer of electrons.

As discussed above, we demonstrated that NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites exhibit strong eliminating ability to hydroxyl radicals, superoxide and ABTS free radicals, and show same trend in both concentration and NCDs loading content dependence. This suggests the tunable and efficient antioxidant capability of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites. Oxidative stress caused by excessive free radicals can cause irreversible damage to the body, leading to various diseases. NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites are expected to serve as an ideal alternative to antioxidant for effectively regulating free radical content in organisms.

Light induced oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites were evaluated using TMB as a substrate (Fig. 5 ). It appears that after 20 min illumination, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites can accelerate the oxidation of substrate TMB, generating the blue product. UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed that the characteristic peak of the reacted solution was at 652 nm, indicating the formation of the oxidized TMB (oxTMB) (Fig. 5a). For the control samples without NCDs/UiO-66 or light, no evident oxidation of TMB is observed from the absorption and color production. These results preliminarily proved the photo-triggered oxidase activity of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites. The light induced oxidase-like catalytic activity of NCDs/UiO-66 not only depends on the catalyst concentration and type, but also on a variety of external factors, including light source, solution pH, temperature and substrate concentration. Fig. 5b compares the oxidase-like activities of different samples in various lighting conditions. It seems that NC/UiO-3 always shows the highest performance regardless of the changing lighting conditions. The order of catalytic property is: NC/UiO-3 > NC/UiO-2 > NC/UiO-1. The catalytic activity of pure UiO-66 and pure NCDs appears quite weak. UiO-66 shows to be an inert substance without any reactions under light-activated condition. The catalytic activity of pure NCDs is almost negligible due to its concentration limit. When NCDs are integrated on another metal-organic framework ZIF (zeolitic imidazolate frameworks), no oxidase-like activity was observed. Such result is possibly ascribed to the smaller surface area and less pore structures of ZIF, compared with UiO-66. It should also be noticed that although the light of λ = 520 nm and λ = 700 nm could stimulate the oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites, a better catalytic performance tends to appear under the light-activated condition of λ > 420 nm. Moreover, the influence of pH value on the oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 were generalized, shown in Fig. 5c. With the pH value varying from 1.68 to 10.0, the optimal pH seems to locate at 4.0. The effect of pH on the light-activated oxidase-like activity is similar than that on natural enzymes and previously reported nanozymes [30–32]. The pH-dependent effect is probably due to the diamine structure of TMB which shows poor solubility in a weak base medium [33]. The oxidase-like activity tends to be promoted with the increasing of irradiation time and the catalyst concentration (Fig. 5d-f). In order to further investigate the kinetics of NCDs/UiO-66 in light-activated condition, the effect of TMB concentration on the catalytic activity was investigated. The catalytic rate seems to increase with the rising of TMB concentration (Fig. 5e). The Michaelis-Menten curve was introduced to theoretically evaluate the enzyme kinetic characteristics. According to the calculating results, Vmax (maximum reaction rate) reached a value of 1.72 × 10−8 M s−1 and Km (Michaelis constant) was calculated to be 0.05 mM (Fig. 5f). Km is considered as an indicator of affinity between enzyme and substrate. A lower Km value indicated a higher affinity for the substrate. Therefore, the low Km obtained above indicate that NCDs/UiO-66 hybrid nanozyme has high affinity for TMB and good oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-induced generation of reactive oxygen species
ce:section.ce:para: ESR spectroscopy combined with spin trap technology confirmed that NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites can generate reactive oxygen species under light irradiation. Representative spin traps are DMPO and TEMP, which are used to capture hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide (O2 •−) and singlet oxygen (1O2). Fig. 6 compares the ESR spectra of solutions containing spin traps and NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites under dark condition or under light with wavelengths above 420 nm. Samples in dark condition and samples containing only spin traps showed no detectable ESR signals (Fig. 6a). In contrast, for samples containing NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites in light condition, characteristic ESR signals of BMPO and TEMP were observed under visible light irradiation for 5 min (Fig. 6b). The four-line spectrum with a relative intensity of 1:2:2:1 is a typical ESR signal of DMPO/•OH adduct. The four-line signal with a relative intensity of 1:1:1:1 is the signal characteristic to DMPO/ O2 •− adduct. The three-line spectrum with a relative intensity of 1:1:1 is the characteristic spectrum of TEMPONE formed by the reaction of TEMP and 1O2 [34]. These results prove that NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites could effectively promote the generation of hydroxyl radical, superoxide, and singlet oxygen under visible light stimulation. It is well known that absorption of light having energy similar to the band gap of a semiconductor results in charge separation and creation of electron-hole pairs, which can react with surrounding molecular oxygen or water to form various ROS. The electrons trapped transiently on the surface or on the next-to surface defects can react with the adsorbed oxygen molecules forming O2 •− and hole can react with the H2O molecules forming •OH. Moreover, previous study [16] has proposed that superoxide anion is involved in the generation of singlet oxygen, implying that electron transfer is an intermediate step for generation of singlet oxygen by photoexcited NCDs/UiO-66. This phenomenon further reveals the essential mechanism of the light-induced transformation from antioxidant to pro-oxidant activity of NCDs/UiO-66.

Based on the above results and analysis. Conclusions could be interpretated that under dark, the abundant aromatic conjugated structure and surface OH groups endow the NCDs/UiO-66 with strong ability to donate electrons thus result in reduction of free radicals. Under irradiation, NCDs/UiO-66 with semiconducting property can absorb the light with equivalent energy and be excited to produce electron/hole (e-/h+) pairs, electrons in valence band (VB) is promoted to conduction band (CB), leaving holes in VB. Photogenerated electrons with strong reducing ability can react with oxygen to produce superoxide radicals, while the hole with oxidative ability to oxidize oxygen containing species (e.g·H2O, OH–), O2 and superoxide to generate hydroxyl radical (•OH) and 1O2. We summarize the mechanisms underlying antioxidant and light induced pro-oxidant activity of NCDs/UiO-66 in Fig. 6c. It is proposed that the antioxidant activity of NCDs/UiO-66 is mainly determined by electron transfer process while the light-activated pro-oxidant activity is dominated by the photo-excitation mechanism [35,36]. This unique property will make NCDs/UiO-66 greatly potential in design of light-responsive materials for use in anti and pro-oxidant applications.

Because of unique fluorescent response and light-activated oxidase-like activity, NCDs/UiO-66 composites may possess promising multi-functions in the field of chemical and biological sensing. To prove this concept, the detection of Fe3+ is firstly developed based on its linear influence on the fluorescence (FL) emission of NCDs/UiO-66 composites. As discussed above, under excitation with light λ = 360 nm, NCDs/UiO-66 shows the characteristic fluorescence emission peak of at 440 nm. A variety of metal cations (e.g. Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ni+, Cu2+, Zn2+) and anions (e.g. Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-) were investigated for their effect on the FL emission of NCDs/UiO-66 (Fig. 7 a and 7b). It was found that among all the tested cations and anions, only the addition of Fe3+ can significantly quench the fluorescence while other ions cause negligible change comparable to the blank control group. This is probably generated by two mechanisms of interaction between Fe3+ and NCDS: (1) Fe3+ directly reacts with NCDs to form Fe2+. (2) Fe3+ interacts with the surface functional groups of NCDs through electrostatic interaction to form a complex NCDs/Fe3+ with no fluorescence [37]. The quenching process may include both static quenching and dynamic quenching, which will result in non-radiative electron transferring from excited NCDs/UiO-66 to the 3d orbit of Fe3+. Fig. 7c shows the effect of Fe3+ with different concentrations on the fluorescence emission at 440 nm of NCDs/UiO-66. A good linear relationship is found between the FL intensity variance and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0 to 0.1 mM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. As the concentration of Fe3+ gradually increases from 0.1 mM to 0.5 mM, the fluorescence intensity distinctly decreases, indicating greatly quenching of fluorescence emission (Fig. 7d). In summary, NCDs/UiO-66 could be used as a selective fluorescent probe to Fe3+, its actual application in biological and environmental detection is expected in future study.

Glutathione (GSH) is one of the most common antioxidants in the organism. It could protect the sulfhydryl groups in many molecules, such as proteins and enzymes from being oxidized by harmful substances and ensure the normal function of them. Abnormal levels of GSH in human cells are usually associated with various diseases, including liver damage, cancer, immunodeficiency virus (HIV) or Parkinson's disease. Considering that GSH has reducing ability to ROS, the light induced oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66 may be inhibited by GSH. Based on this point, a colorimetric assay for GSH detection was designed by employing the oxidase-like activity of NCDs/UiO-66. As demonstrated above, NCDs/UiO-66 can quickly catalyze the oxidation of TMB under irradiation, showing a typical blue evolution. It is found that addition of GSH can efficiently inhibit the TMB oxidation in a concentration dependent manner. As shown in Fig. 8 a, increasing the dosage of GSH from 0 to 40 μM result in a gradual reduction of the absorption corresponding to the TMB oxidation products, indicating the inhibitory degree is closely related to the concentration of GSH (Fig. 8a and 8b). Fig. 8c plots the absorbance variation at 652 nm in the function of GSH concentration, a linear relationship was found in the range from 0 to 15 μM. The limit of detection is calculated to be 0.48 μM (S = 3σ), revealing the high sensitivity for GSH detection. In order to further evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, several common biological species such as glucose, histidine, uric acid, glycine and l-cysteine were introduced in the experiment (Fig. 8d). Most of them have little influence on the detection for GSH, expect for l-cysteine. In fact, the concentration of GSH (0.5–10 mM) is much higher than that of l-cysteine (30–200 μM) in human body, thus the interference from l-cysteine could be ignored. Therefore, NCDs/UiO-66 demonstrates excellent anti-interference performance as a colorimetric probe for GSH detection.

On basis of the previously reported preparative approaches [16,24,38], this work has demonstrated that NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites show light-controlled crossover of redox ability. Under dark condition, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites have efficient anti-oxidant capability to scavenge ROS and free radicals. While under light irradiation, NCDs/UiO-66 can produce ROS and exhibit oxidase-like activity, showing pro-oxidant capability. The anti-oxidant and pro-oxidant ability are dependent on the structure and concentration of NCDs/UiO-66. Furthermore, the anti-oxidant and pro-oxidant ability seems to be dominated by electron transfer and photoinduced production of electron/hole (e-/h+) pairs. Compared with the previous reports [16,37], NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites overcome the defects of carbon dos which are difficult to collect and purify, and further improve the biological stability. From the perspective of application, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites show high sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+ and GSH. Therefore, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites are potential to play a greater role in biological detection. In addition, NCDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites are expected to be designed as a responsive nanomedicine to improve photodynamic therapy and treat oxidative stress diseases [37,38]. In conclusion, this research provides a new design idea for constructing light-activated nanocomposites, which may break through the bottleneck of unidirectional adjustment in enzymes and broaden the applications of nanocatalytic materials.

Jun Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jiawen Gong: Investigation, Data curation. Jingnan Wei: Investigation, Data curation. Qi Yang: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Guangjie Li: Visualization, Data curation. Yuping Tong: Supervision, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Weiwei He: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772256 and 21805085) and the Program for Zhongyuan Leading Talents of Science and Technology Innovation in Henan Province (204200510016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.03.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
